1 Introduction

This report summarizes the pilot survey for a study on AI memory and platform lock-in. The survey elicits beliefs about AI memory, willingness to accept (WTA) compensation for memory erasure, and willingness to pay (WTP) for a competing AI assistant under varying data portability scenarios. These measures map directly onto a theoretical model of switching costs driven by accumulated personalization data.

The pilot was fielded on Prolific with N = 206 respondents who completed the survey in a median of 5.2 minutes. All WTP/WTA questions were asked within-subjects (the full experiment will use a between-subjects design).

Platform usage: personal, work, and paid subscriptions

Work Use
Paid
Platform N % N % N %

ChatGPT dominates personal use (77%) and work use. 28% of respondents pay for any AI subscription. The mean respondent uses 2.3 platforms, and 65% qualify as multihomers (2+ platforms).

AI Platform Usage (Personal vs. Work)

Usage Frequency

Regarding multihoming frequency (Q4): Always (1%), Never (24%), Often (12%), Rarely (28%), Sometimes (35%).

Number of AI Platforms Used

2.3 Ad-Free Valuation

WTP for Ad-Free AI

Ad Sentiment

Mean ad sentiment is 2.1 on a 1–7 scale (1 = strongly dislike). The majority (62%) would rather see ads than pay, despite strongly disliking ads.

3 Memory Beliefs and Switching Perceptions

3.1 Memory Awareness

Memory Awareness Crosstab
AI Remembers? Reviewed Memories? N %
I’m not sure I didn’t know I could 8 3.9
I’m not sure No 26 12.6
I’m not sure Yes 3 1.5
No I didn’t know I could 6 2.9
No No 10 4.9
No Yes 9 4.4
Yes I didn’t know I could 16 7.8
Yes No 67 32.5
Yes Yes 61 29.6

70% of respondents believe their AI remembers them. Only 35% have reviewed their AI’s memories, and 15% did not know they could.

Memory Beliefs Overview

Perceived improvement from memory averages 4.6 / 7 (SD = 1.6), indicating moderate perceived value.

3.2 Switching Losses

Perceived Switching Losses

30% report “nothing significant” would be lost from switching. The most common concern is losing preferences/style, followed by conversation history.

3.3 Time to Re-Personalize

Conversations Needed to Re-Personalize

5% believe a new assistant would never fully catch up—an extreme form of perceived lock-in.

4 Valuation Results

4.1 Survey-to-Model Mapping

Mapping: Survey Variables to Model Notation
Survey Q Variable Model Description
Q12 wta_memory_erasure W1 WTA to erase ChatGPT memory
Q13 wtp_claude_no_strings W2 WTP for Claude (memory intact)
Q14 wtp_claude_with_erasure W4 WTP for Claude (memory erased)
Q15 wtp_claude_with_transfer W3 WTP for Claude (memory transferred)
Q13 - Q14 demand_suppression W2 - W4 Demand suppression from memory
Q15 - Q14 portability_recovery W3 - W4 Demand recovered by portability
1-(Q13-Q15)/(Q13-Q14) phi_eff phi_eff Effective portability share

4.2 WTA for Memory Erasure

WTA for Permanent Memory Erasure

Valuation Summary Statistics
Measure N % at $0 Mean Median SD IQR
WTA memory erasure (W1) 203 13.8 162.7 50 284.8 95
WTP Claude no strings (W2) 204 55.4 4.7 0 7.3 5
WTP Claude with erasure (W4) 204 66.7 3.9 0 8.2 5
WTP Claude with transfer (W3) 204 49.0 6.1 5 8.2 10

4.3 WTP Across Scenarios

WTP for Claude Pro: Within-Subject Comparison

Demand Curves and Margin Decomposition

The demand curves show the gap between scenarios: the vertical distance between the “no strings” and “with erasure” curves represents demand suppression, while the distance from “with transfer” to “with erasure” represents portability recovery.

4.4 Demand Suppression and Portability

Demand suppression (Q13 \(-\) Q14): mean = $1.21, SD = $6.7, t-test against zero: p = 0.0108.

Portability recovery (Q15 \(-\) Q14): mean = $2.46, SD = $6.85, t-test against zero: p = 6.91e-07.

Effective portability (\(\hat{\varphi}_{eff}\)): median = 1, mean = 1.02 (N = 50 with nonzero denominator).

Consistency check: 74.3% of respondents have W2 \(\geq\) W3 \(\geq\) W4 (theory-consistent ordering); 96% have W2 \(\geq\) W4.

Sensitivity: Censoring Assumptions
Scenario WTA Mean WTP No Strings WTP Erasure WTP Transfer
Baseline (excl. censored) 162.7 4.7 3.9 6.1
Censor ‘No amount’ at $1,000 174.9 5.4 4.6 6.8
Censor ‘No amount’ at $2,000 189.4 5.4 4.6 6.8

4.5 WTA by Perceived Improvement

Mean WTA by Perceived Improvement Level

5 Mechanisms

5.1 Demand Suppression and Usage Intensity

If memory and usage are complements (\(u_{tm} > 0\)), heavier users should exhibit greater demand suppression. We regress (Q13 \(-\) Q14) on usage frequency, controlling for demographics.

OLS: Demand Suppression on Usage Frequency + Demographics
Term Estimate SE t p
(Intercept) 3.093 2.351 1.315 0.190
freq_num 0.501 0.448 1.118 0.265

age_group35-44 -2.809 1.832 -1.533 0.127
age_group45-54 -5.073 1.936 -2.620 0.010
age_group55-64 -2.404 2.275 -1.056 0.292
age_group65+ -3.700 2.510 -1.474 0.142
genderNon-binary -2.470 5.304 -0.466 0.642
genderWoman -0.468 0.981 -0.476 0.634
educationDoctoral or professional degree 2.739 4.293 0.638 0.524
educationHigh school or less -3.111 1.483 -2.097 0.037
educationMaster’s degree -0.122 1.519 -0.080 0.936
educationSome college 1.066 1.154 0.924 0.357

Demand Suppression by Usage Frequency

5.2 Memory Awareness vs. Perceived Improvement

WTA by Memory Awareness and Perceived Improvement

This 2$$2 decomposition tests the “behavioral wedge”: do respondents who are unaware their AI remembers them but nonetheless rate memory as valuable exhibit different WTA? If so, WTA may reflect general aversion to data loss rather than experienced personalization value.

5.3 Concentration Beliefs vs. Actual Multihoming

Concentration Beliefs vs. Actual Multihoming

6 Design Calibration

This section summarizes findings relevant to the full experiment design.

6.1 BDM Price Range

The WTA distribution for memory erasure (Q12) suggests:

  • 14% accept $0 (no value from memory)
  • The 25th–75th percentiles span $5 to $100
  • 1.5% say “no amount” (right-censored)

Recommendation: A BDM price list from $0 to $100 in $5 increments would capture the bulk of the distribution while maintaining incentive compatibility. Consider a broader range ($0–$500) for the WTA question if budget permits.

6.2 Stratification Candidates

Covariates Predicting WTA (p < 0.10)
Term Estimate SE t p
frequency_ordered.L 120.572 48.011 2.511 0.013
ai_remembersYes -114.900 60.649 -1.895 0.060
perceived_improvement_num 34.197 15.892 2.152 0.033

6.3 Preliminary Effect Sizes

For power calculations:

  • Demand suppression (Q13 \(-\) Q14): Cohen’s \(d\) = 0.181
  • Portability recovery (Q15 \(-\) Q14): Cohen’s \(d\) = 0.36

These within-subject effect sizes serve as upper bounds for the between-subjects design. The between-subjects experiment should plan for effect sizes approximately 50–70% of these values.

7 Appendix

7.1 Survey Completion Time

Completion Time (minutes)
Min. 1st Qu. Median Mean 3rd Qu. Max.
2.2 4.2 5.2 6 6.9 25.3

7.2 Awareness of Claude Import Feature

6.8% were aware of Claude’s ChatGPT memory import feature before the survey. Among those aware (N = 14), 3 had actually used it.

7.3 Forecast: Quality After Erasure

Mean forecast quality after one month of re-use: 3.9 / 7 (1 = substantially worse, 7 = substantially better). Respondents expect moderate degradation but not catastrophic loss.

7.4 Forecast: Transfer Fidelity

Mean expected transfer fidelity: 56%. Distribution: 0%: 20, 100%: 29, 25%: 33, 50%: 56, 75%: 68.
